gold.rationale: The article’s main explanatory point about the March 16 tornado watch and the March 6 Michigan supercell is grounded in real weather reporting and the cited Michigan storm-chaser survey details. However, it mildly overstates the wider East Coast disruption picture, uses an edited header graphic, and pairs the text with authentic but context-shifted damage imagery that is not from the March 16 Mid-Atlantic event.

retrieved_evidence.text: The Union City EF‑3 tornado documented in Michigan on March 6 has been cited by meteorologists as a clear example of how a non‑watched storm can still become extremely destructive. The official survey card for Union City lists a long, relatively wide track with estimated peak winds in the EF‑3 range, along with notations on injuries, any fatalities and structural impacts along the path. In recent analyses, this event is used to emphasize that dangerous tornadic storms may form rapidly, sometimes outside existing watch areas.

Full media release for details and maps of the four tornadoes that touched down on March 6th, 2026. Details of each tornado are not likely to change at this point, however all surveys are preliminary until official publication by the NWS into their database at the end of the year. Additional tornadoes will not be added from March 6th, 2026 unless a collection of convincing information comes in for survey. Here are the details in order.

- Edwardsburg EF-1, max winds of 95 mph, 350 yards wide, 13.4 miles long, 0 injured, 1 deceased
- Three Rivers EF-2, max winds of 130 mph, 450 yards wide, 10.75 miles long, 10 injured, 0 deceased
- Union City EF-3, max winds of 160 mph, 500 yards wide, 5.23 miles long, 12 injured, 3 deceased
- Tekonsha EF-0, max winds of 85 mph, 125 yards wide, 2.75 miles long, 0 injured, 0 deceased

All four tornadoes came from the same parent supercell, resulting in a total of 22 injured, and 4 deceased, along with an estimated tens of millions of dollars in real-estate damage. Our hearts are still with those who lost family members on this day.

As an added "what happened?" moment, we can tell you that this supercell was all by itself, ahead of the main complex of storms that occurred later in the day. Because of a multitude of factors including storm isolation, this lone storm did not meet the criteria of a tornado watch. It was still, however, tornado warned by the National Weather Service for nearly its entire lifespan when it finally showed signs of rotation. Similar to severe thunderstorm watches, not all storms will get this watch ahead of a warning due to preset criteria. It's very important to check the forecasts on severe weather days, so you can be prepared to take action, and have multiple ways to receive warnings when warnings are finally issued. We will go more in depth on watch criteria in a future post or video, as we always do our best to educate when it comes to weather terminology. Tomorrow, March 18th, 2026 is also Michigan's statewide tornado drill day, so be prepared for test alerts from your city's sirens and WEA's on your phone. Take tomorrow to comb over your severe weather plan ahead of the next severe day in Michigan.

In recent reports on the active March severe‑weather pattern, storm‑survey documentation from Edwardsburg, Michigan, has been used to illustrate how meteorologists catalogue tornado events in the Great Lakes region. A typical National Weather Service survey card summarizes details such as track length, maximum width, estimated peak winds and the timing of the tornadic circulation, allowing officials to link the Edwardsburg tornado to a broader series of spring storms that have produced watches and warnings from the Midwest to the East Coast.

Amateur meteorologists from a group known as Michigan Storm Chasers reported that four tornadoes touched down on March 6, 2026, all associated with a single supercell thunderstorm that moved across southern parts of the state. According to their preliminary account, the events were categorized as an EF-1 near Edwardsburg, an EF-2 near Three Rivers, an EF-3 near Union City and an EF-0 near Tekonsha, reflecting varying intensities along the storm’s track. Local authorities said survey teams and national weather officials would review the observations and damage patterns before final classifications are confirmed.

The four tornadoes reported in southern Michigan on March 6, 2026, resulted in a combined toll of 22 injuries and 4 deaths, according to early figures compiled by local officials and volunteer groups. Damage assessments indicate that losses likely reach into the tens of millions of dollars, with homes, farm buildings and sections of critical infrastructure among the affected properties. Emergency managers said that some residents were displaced and shelter operations were activated in several communities, while insurance adjusters and government agencies continue to document the full scope of the impact.

National Weather Service survey teams follow a standardized damage‑assessment protocol after events like the March 6 Michigan tornadoes, systematically documenting structural impacts, vegetation damage and debris patterns along each track. These field notes, narrative accounts and mapped paths are later consolidated into survey cards, such as those for Edwardsburg and Union City, which determine the Enhanced Fujita (EF) rating. In recent briefings, officials have explained that such documentation not only quantifies intensity but also informs building‑code discussions and risk communication for future storms.

Meteorologists reviewing the severe weather of March 6, 2026, in Michigan say the supercell that produced multiple tornadoes appeared as an isolated storm ahead of a larger complex, according to an internal summary circulated this month. The account indicates that, based on prevailing criteria and available data at the time, a broader tornado watch was not in effect for the area, even though the storm itself was under a tornado warning for much of its lifespan once rotation signatures were detected on radar. Officials noted that such cases are being examined to refine guidance and communication with local partners.

Emergency‑preparedness guidance circulated after the March tornadoes in Michigan and the issuance of Tornado Watch 67 along the East Coast has stressed that residents should rely on multiple alert methods. Authorities recommend combining NOAA Weather Radio, wireless emergency alerts and local media so that households can receive timely notice of warnings, whether or not a tornado watch is currently in effect. According to public safety officials, having a pre‑identified interior shelter and a family plan remains crucial during the volatile spring storm season.

A Tornado Watch numbered 67 was issued on March 16 for portions of the Mid-Atlantic region as a vigorous storm system tracked from the Carolinas toward Virginia and Maryland, according to early statements from the National Weather Service. Forecasters said the watch covered several states for a defined afternoon and evening period, highlighting a risk of damaging winds, large hail and possible tornadoes along and ahead of an advancing cold front. Local emergency managers in affected counties urged residents to monitor updated bulletins and to be prepared to move to shelter quickly if conditions deteriorated or additional warnings were posted.

Rural communities in states such as Michigan and across the broader Great Lakes corridor have recently faced scattered destruction from fast‑moving supercell thunderstorms. Under overcast, gray skies, damaged outbuildings, snapped trees and mangled farm equipment have become a common sight in local accounts, underscoring how even a brief tornado touchdown can leave families and small towns coping with debris removal, temporary displacement and infrastructure repairs well after the storm has passed.

Tornado Watch 67 was issued for parts of the Mid-Atlantic on Monday, March 16, as a strong storm system pushed severe thunderstorms from the Carolinas toward Virginia and Maryland. Residents were urged to stay weather-aware.

Weather agencies in the United States continue to emphasize the distinction between tornado watches and tornado warnings as part of their public safety messaging, officials said this week. A tornado watch is issued when atmospheric conditions are considered favorable for tornadoes and severe thunderstorms over a relatively broad region and time frame, indicating that people should remain alert. By contrast, a tornado warning is reserved for smaller, more specific areas when radar data or confirmed sightings suggest that a tornado is occurring or is imminent, prompting immediate protective action from those in its path.

Forecasters at the Storm Prediction Center (SPC) and local National Weather Service offices issued Tornado Watch 67 on March 16 for portions of the Mid‑Atlantic as a vigorous low‑pressure system tracked north and east. The official watch outline, drawn as a polygon over state boundaries, indicated where conditions were favorable for tornado development over several hours. According to meteorologists, this type of watch highlights an elevated risk zone but does not guarantee that every tornadic storm will remain confined within its boundaries.

Local governments in affected Michigan counties and Mid‑Atlantic jurisdictions placed under Tornado Watch 67 have activated emergency‑operations centers to coordinate response during this active March pattern. According to county officials, tasks include opening temporary shelters, managing power‑outage reports and dispatching public‑works crews to clear fallen trees from roads. The recent sequence of events has also prompted after‑action reviews focused on siren activation timing, interagency coordination and the effectiveness of public messaging about tornado threats outside formal watch areas.

Residential neighborhoods struck by severe thunderstorms or embedded tornadoes often show highly localized impact, with some homes largely intact while adjacent structures sustain heavy harm. Reports from recent Michigan storms have described siding and roofing punctured by airborne debris, shattered windows and broken fences, illustrating how small projectiles propelled by tornadic winds can penetrate exterior walls. According to emergency‑management officials, such concentrated damage patterns help survey teams distinguish between straight‑line winds and true tornado paths.

In recent days, millions of residents along stretches of the East Coast were reported to be under various levels of severe-weather alerts as a series of storms swept through the region, according to emergency officials. The system brought episodes of heavy rain, strong gusts and embedded thunderstorms, which led to scattered power outages and delays at major transportation hubs. Airlines and rail operators cited safety concerns and congestion as they adjusted schedules, while local authorities warned that lingering travel disruptions and cleanup operations could continue in the following days in some of the hardest-hit communities.

Millions across the East Coast faced weather disruptions Monday, March 16, with tornado watches posted in multiple states as a sweeping storm system moved through. Flight delays piled up as strong winds and storms hit major hubs.

text_analysis.alignment_reasoning: The post accurately details the March 6 Michigan tornado outbreak statistics and the March 16 Mid-Atlantic watch, but it presents the March 6 event as a confirmed historical fact with precise numbers and specific attribution to 'Michigan Storm Chasers' without the necessary qualifiers found in the evidence regarding preliminary survey status.
text_analysis.t1_evidence: The post states 'Meteorologists often describe severe-weather forecasting as a nesting set of probabilities... The combined toll was devastating—22 injuries and four deaths—with damage estimated in the tens of millions of dollars' as established fact, whereas evidence [1] explicitly states 'all surveys are preliminary until official publication by the NWS into their database at the end of the year' and 'Details of each tornado are not likely to change at this point'.
t1_problematic_subclaims: The post presents the March 6 Michigan tornado survey results (22 injuries, 4 deaths, specific wind speeds) as definitive historical fact without noting they are preliminary.
The post attributes the report to 'Michigan Storm Chasers' as a definitive source for the final numbers, whereas evidence [2] and [4] indicate these were preliminary accounts subject to review.
The post claims the March 16 Tornado Watch 67 was issued at 10:50 a.m. EDT, a specific timestamp not corroborated by the evidence which only confirms the watch was issued on March 16.
text_analysis.global_assessment: The post is largely accurate regarding the March 16 event but dangerously overstates the certainty of the March 6 Michigan tornado data by omitting critical qualifiers about the preliminary nature of the surveys.

judge.level3_rationale: 该帖子准确描述了2026年3月6日密歇根州龙卷风爆发事件的具体统计数据（如伤亡人数、风速、路径长度）以及3月16日中大西洋地区的龙卷风预警情况，所提供的图像（包括详细的调查数据图表和风暴破坏照片）与文本内容完全一致且得到证据支持，没有任何虚构、扭曲或语境错置。
judge.reasoning_trace: The post's claims are fully corroborated by the retrieved evidence (social media release, news snippets, fact briefs) and the provided images match the described events perfectly.
judge.core_claim: The post explains the difference between tornado watches and warnings using the March 6, 2026 Michigan tornado outbreak as a case study, supported by preliminary survey data and images of storm damage.
key_subclaims: The March 6, 2026 Michigan tornado outbreak involved four tornadoes (EF-1, EF-2, EF-3, EF-0) from a single supercell.
The outbreak resulted in 22 injuries and 4 deaths.
The Edwardsburg tornado was an EF-1 with 95 mph winds, 350 yards width, 13.4 miles path, 0 injuries, 1 death.
The Union City tornado was an EF-3 with 160 mph winds, 500 yards width, 5.23 miles path, 12 injuries, 3 deaths.
The March 16 Mid-Atlantic event featured Tornado Watch 67.
The images provided show the preliminary survey results for the Edwardsburg and Union City tornadoes and photos of storm damage.
